coredata.dc:title: Development of ionic liquid-capped carbon dots derived from Tecoma stans (L.) Juss. ex Kunth: Combatting bacterial pathogens in diabetic foot ulcer pus swabs, targeting both standard and multi-drug resistant strains

coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infection is rapidly growing and is a severe danger to global welfare worldwide. Consequently, there is a growing demand for novel antibacterial medications. As an outcome, the evaluation of antibacterial assets of carbon dots (CDs) including a variety of ionic liquids (ILs) were employed to reside advance notice of the structure tunability of ILs along with abundant surface functional group of designed amine-modified CDs, have recently sparked a lot of interest. In this study for the first time, highly efficient ionic-liquid decorated CDs were synthesized to assess the impact of capped-ionic liquid on the degree of antibacterial activity. Fluorescent amine-modified CDs (CDs-NH2) were obtained from the Tecoma stans (yellow elder flower) extract by a single-step carbonization method in presence of ethylenediamine, and then capped with ionic liquids; 3-(carboxymethyl)-1-methyl-1-H-imidazol-3-ium bromide (ILIM) and 3-(carboxymethyl)-1-methyl-1-H-benzo[d]imidazole-3-ium bromide (ILBIM) by a simple carbodiimide chemistry. In-vitro antibacterial activity of CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were evaluated against common pathogens, Escherichia coli (E. coli) as Gram(-ive) bacteria and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) as Gram(+ive) bacteria as well as against clinical multi-drug resistant strain obtained from pus swab of diabetic foot ulcer, by microdilution method as well as well-diffusion method. The results showed that functional ILs-capped CDs have enhanced antibacterial activity against standard (E. coli, S. aureus) and clinical resistant bacterial strains, with minimal inhibitory concentrations (MICs) values range from 0 to 64 μg/mL for standard E. coli/ S. aureus and 250 μg/mL for drug resistant clinical strains, respectively. Structure activity analysis and molecular docking studies revealed that the antibacterial activity of ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs is particularly better compared to CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquids.

Structural and Morphological characterization (a) PXRD pattern; SEM images of ionic-liquid capped CDs at 200 nm (b & c); TEM image of ionic-liquid capped CDs at 200 nm (d); TEM images of CDs-NH2 (e) and ionic-liquid capped CDs at 5 nm (f).
FTIR spectrum of yellow-elder extract (YE), CDs-NH2, ionic-liquids (ILIM and ILBIM) and ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs).

Elemental composition characterization (a) Raman spectra; (b) survey-scan of synthesized CDs-NH2 and ILBIM@CDs; high resolution C1s (c); N1s (d); O1s (e); spectrum of CDs-NH2; high resolution C1s (f); N1s (g); O1s (h) spectrum of ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILBIM@CDs).

Optical behavior of synthesized CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquid capped CDs; (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of ionic liquids (ILIM and ILBIM), CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs); (b) fluorescence spectra of ionic liquids (ILIM and ILBIM), CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs); (c) & (d) wavelength tuned emission of CDs-NH2 and ILBIM@CDs, respectively; (e) CDs-NH2 and ILBIM@CDs color shifts in buffers at various pH-levels captured on camera using UV light (365 nm); (f) variation of fluorescence intensity at different pH values.

Schematic representation of the binding mode of ILBIM@CDs(a); ILIM@CDs (c); CDs-NH2 (e) and ridinilazole (g) [green]; essential residues are marked with a gray stick in the DNA Gyrase receptor active site; In the DNA Gyrase receptor active site, a Ligplot of ILBIM@CDs (b), ILIM@CDs (d), CDs-NH2 (f), and Ridinilazole (h) is displayed.

The binding mode of ILBIM@CDs (a); ILIM@CDs (c); CDs-NH2 (e) and Ridinilazole (g) [green] is shown in the SA-DHFR active site and important residues are highlighted with gray stick; Ligplot of ILBIM@CDs(b); ILIM@CDs (d); CDs-NH2 (f) and Ridinilazole (h) the active site of the SA-DHFR receptor is shown.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infection is rapidly growing and is a severe danger to global welfare worldwide. Consequently, there is a growing demand for novel antibacterial medications. As an outcome, the evaluation of antibacterial assets of carbon dots (CDs) including a variety of ionic liquids (ILs) were employed to reside advance notice of the structure tunability of ILs along with abundant surface functional group of designed amine-modified CDs, have recently sparked a lot of interest. In this study for the first time, highly efficient ionic-liquid decorated CDs were synthesized to assess the impact of capped-ionic liquid on the degree of antibacterial activity. Fluorescent amine-modified CDs (CDs-NH2) were obtained from the Tecoma stans (yellow elder flower) extract by a single-step carbonization method in presence of ethylenediamine, and then capped with ionic liquids; 3-(carboxymethyl)-1-methyl-1-H-imidazol-3-ium bromide (ILIM) and 3-(carboxymethyl)-1-methyl-1-H-benzo[d]imidazole-3-ium bromide (ILBIM) by a simple carbodiimide chemistry. In-vitro antibacterial activity of CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were evaluated against common pathogens, Escherichia coli (E. coli) as Gram(-ive) bacteria and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) as Gram(+ive) bacteria as well as against clinical multi-drug resistant strain obtained from pus swab of diabetic foot ulcer, by microdilution method as well as well-diffusion method. The results showed that functional ILs-capped CDs have enhanced antibacterial activity against standard (E. coli, S. aureus) and clinical resistant bacterial strains, with minimal inhibitory concentrations (MICs) values range from 0 to 64 μg/mL for standard E. coli/ S. aureus and 250 μg/mL for drug resistant clinical strains, respectively. Structure activity analysis and molecular docking studies revealed that the antibacterial activity of ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs is particularly better compared to CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquids.

ce:section.ce:para: Infectious diseases consistently contribute to more than 25 % of the annual global mortality rate. Emerging infectious diseases have significantly impacted both public health and economies on a global scale, with a particularly pronounced effect on developing nations.(Chatterjee and Mishra, 2020) The incidence of these diseases is on the rise, posing an escalating challenge to global health. The transmission of these diseases is influenced by a multitude of factors, encompassing bacterial pathogens, host factors, and environmental variables, with bacterial pathogens emerging as the predominant agents responsible for numerous infectious diseases.(Cassini et al., 2019; Chatterjee et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2023a) One of these leading causes of mortality today is nosocomial infections, which account for over 37,000 fatalities annually in the US and are on the rise globally. These infections often occur because of the dissemination of the patient's normal bacteria during or after medical treatments. Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) with multi-drug resistance are significant contributors, resulting in severe and sometimes deadly infections like catheter-related urinary tract and surgical site infections. This demonstrates the essential need for potent new antibacterial drugs to combat these pervasive and important nosocomial infection sources (Cassini et al., 2019; Jenkins, 2017; Tacconelli et al., 2018).

Nanoparticles have various applications in the field of medicine, including drug delivery, catalysis (Kumar et al., 2023a; Mishra et al., 2014a; Mudgal et al., 2023a; N. Yadav et al., 2022a), dyes scavengers (Mudgal et al., 2023b; Panda et al., 2021) and encapsulation (Yadav et al., 2022b). In recent investigations, nanomaterials have been discovered to be effective antibacterial agents. The precise control over nanoscale systems allows for the customization of biocompatible nanomedicines. These nanomedicines offer prolonged circulation, reduced immunogenicity, improved tolerability, and enhanced dispersibility (Akram et al., 2022; Hemeg, 2017; Rao et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2014). Nanoparticles exhibit efficacy in combating bacterial infections due to their ability to penetrate bacterial cells and disrupt crucial physiological processes. This provides novel strategies to combat the escalating issue of drug-resistant microorganisms (Majeed et al., 2019; Sasidharan et al., 2020). For avoiding and treating the development of biofilms by clinically relevant infections, nanotechnology has offered creative solutions.(Kumar et al., 2023b) Recent developments in nanotechnological techniques, such as solid lipid nanoparticles, dendrimers, polymeric nanoparticles, cyclodextrin-based delivery, metallic nanoparticles, functionalized metallic nanoparticles, polymer drug conjugates, and liposomes, may offer useful technological solutions against infectious diseases.(Gupta et al., 2021; Yadav et al., 2022b) Recently, there has been significant interest in carbon dots (CDs), a novel group of fluorescent nanomaterials, owing to their remarkable characteristics such as photostability, adjustable excitation and emission properties, low cytotoxicity, and great bioactivity.(N. Yadav et al., 2023; N. 2022a) Furthermore, there has been a growing emphasis on the use of precursors derived from renewable resources or naturally occurring molecules. This is attributed to their potential to facilitate economically viable and ecologically beneficial procedures (Hsu et al., 2022; Mishra et al., 2023; Mitra et al., 2023). Moreover, the abundance of various functional groups in the bio-inspired green precursors (Kumar et al., 2023c) facilitates the formation of self-passivated and heteroatom-doped carbon dots (CDs), both of which have been seen to progressively influence the final properties of the material. The aforementioned advantages have significantly impacted endeavors to further expand this captivating field by exploring a wide range of environmentally friendly resources (Athinarayanan et al., 2020; Kaur et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2017). Functional CDs, amine-modified CDs (CDs-NH2), are particularly suitable as harmless cytotoxic nanoprobes for fluorescence bioimaging and intracellular monitoring in an extensive spectrum of bacteria and animal cells (Okla et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023). Subsequently due to their fluorescent nature and biocompatibility, CDs are highly utilized for the bioimaging purposes, however rarely used as microbial agent due to strong self-agglomeration effect at high temperature. (N. Yadav et al., 2023; Yadav et al., 2022a) It has been proposed to utilize a specific molecule to stabilize CDs and enable their isolation in powder (Łuczak et al., 2016). Additionally, scientists are highly interested in utilizing ionic liquids too because they have a wide range of uses in the pharmaceutical sector for drug delivery systems and for treating various ailments. A key physicochemical characteristic that governs a drug's destiny at the target location is solubility. Due to low solubility, many potential drug candidates fall short at different stages of drug development (Nikfarjam et al., 2021a; Shukla et al., 2023). Ionic liquids have been postulated as novel stabilizers with reduced cytotoxicity due to their favored reactivity on negative species membranes (bacterial walls and membranes) rather than neutral membranes (mammalian cells). Ionic liquids composed of antimicrobial imidazolium ring as cationic group and biocompatible anion (Br−) are intriguing in terms of health purposes (Pendleton and Gilmore, 2015). Consequently, the ionic composition of ILs influences not only their solubility, but also their physiological functions, toxic effects, and biodegradability. Recent studies have shown that imidazolium-based salts may operate as transmembrane anion transporters, produce an electrolyte imbalance, degrade bacterial integrity and membrane potential, and have low cytotoxicity over the bacteriostatic dose range (Elie et al., 2015; Oh et al., 2014; Son et al., 2018; Yoo et al., 2016).

Antibacterial agents were historically identified and synthesized using natural compounds. Tecoma stans, often known as T. stans, was a perennial shrub belonging to the Bignoniaceae family of trumpet vines. It is native to the Americas and is commonly cultivated as a garden ornament. This plant had many medicinal qualities, such as antioxidant, antiproliferative, wound-healing, cytotoxic, and antibacterial activities. Research had made substantial advancements in the production of organic biogenic CDs-NH2 using a low-cost and environmentally friendly T. stans extract. This paper described the production of CDs-NH2 using Tecoma stans extract and ethylene diamine, followed by further modification using ionic liquids (ILIM and ILBIM) utilizing a simple methodology. The objective of that study was to investigate the possible enhancement of antibacterial effects via the combination of modified CDs and stable ionic liquids, with the potential outcome of lowering the necessary effective dosages. The selection of functionalized CDs was based on many key criteria, including their simplicity, stability over a wide range of pH values, little aggregation, neutral charge, and tiny size, which enabled them to effectively navigate confined biological environments. The resultant product underwent comprehensive characterization via the use of several methodologies. The antibacterial capabilities of the synthesized CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs, and ILBIM@CDs were evaluated against common pathogens such as E. coli and S. aureus, as well as drug-resistant strains, via the use of microdilution and well-diffusion techniques. The CDs that were altered showed significant antibacterial efficacy against both conventional and drug-resistant pathogens. The efficacy of ionic-liquid-capped CDs as antibacterial agents against bacterial infections was further substantiated by in vitro experiments and molecular docking analyses.

The reagents required for the manufacture of ionic liquids (ILs) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, while the solvents were obtained from SD Fine Chemicals. The bacterial strains used in this study were E. coli (ATCC 25923) and S. aureus (ATCC 25922), which were obtained from the Institute of Microbial Technology (IMTECH) in Chandigarh, India. A clinical bacterial strain that is resistant to many drugs was acquired from the Microbiology lab at St. Stephen's Hospital. The strain was collected from a diabetic foot-pus swab sample and has shown resistance to commonly used antibacterial medications available on the market (see Fig. S8; see the supporting information). The nutrition broth (NB) and nutrient agar media were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All experimental procedures and photophysical investigations were conducted using Milli-Q water. The characterization investigations were performed under ambient conditions.

The fresh whole flowering aerial parts including flowers, buds, and leaves of Tecoma stans (L.) Juss. ex Kanth syn. Bignonia stans L. which is commonly known as Yellow Elder, Yellow trumpet flower, Yellow trumpet bush, were collected from “Amity University Noida campus” Uttar Pradesh, India. The sample specimen was identified by Dr. Sunita Garg, Head, Raw Materials Herbarium and Museum, Delhi (RMHD), CSIR-National Institute of Science Communication and Policy Research (NIScPR), Delhi, India. A voucher specimen [NIScPR/RMHD/Consult/2023/4598–99] of leaves, flowers and buds were deposited into the herbarium of RMHD, New Delhi, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosynthesis of amine-rich CDs (CDs-NH2) from Tecoma stans (L.) Juss. ex Kanth (T. stans) extract
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CDs-NH2 was carried out using moderate hydrothermal conditions. In a concise manner, a total volume of 10 mL of T. stans extract was obtained using the filtration process after the crushing of flower petals in a mortar. Additionally, a volume of 300 μL of ethylenediamine was combined with 10 mL of floral extract and agitated for a duration of 10 min in order to get a uniform solution. Subsequently, the amalgamation was enclosed inside a stainless-steel autoclave with a capacity of 40cc, which was internally coated with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). The purpose of this setup was to subject the combination to hydrothermal treatment for a duration of 5 h, maintaining a temperature of 180 °C. Following the cooling of the autoclave to ambient temperature, the resulting suspension underwent centrifugation to eliminate larger particles. The as-synthesized amine-CDs solutions were dialyzed for 72 h using D-Tube Maxi (3 KDa or 1.5 nm filters according to product specifications) dialyzers with Milli-Q water changed every two hours to eliminate unreacted compounds. After being lyophilized (−70 °C) to create a black, sticky product, the resultant supernatant (CDs-NH2) was re-suspended in water as required (Abd Rani et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2019).

General procedure applied for the fabrication of 3-(carboxymethyl)−1-methyl-1 H-imidazol-3-ium bromide (ILIM) and 3-(carboxymethyl)−1-methyl-1 H-benzo[d]imidazole-3-ium bromide (ILBIM). (N. Yadav et al., 2023)

For the synthesis of ILIM , 1-methylimidazole (1.10 mmol) was mixed with 1 equivalent bromoacetic acid in 10 mL CH3CN and refluxed for 6 h with continuous stirring. In a similar manner, 1-methylbenzimidazole was also dissolved in CH3CN followed by addition of 1 equivalent of bromoacetic acid to fabricate ILBIM. After 6 h of refluxing, the isolated ionic liquids (ILIM /ILBIM ) were separated and evaporated, and further characterized via using proton and carbon NMR spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosynthesis of ionic liquid capped CDs-NH2 (ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to create ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs, 20 mg of CDs-NH2 were quickly dissolved in milli-Q water (10 mL), and then coupling reagents EDC.HCl (15 mg) and DMAP (3 mg) were added. The reaction mixture was subjected to agitation for a duration of 1 h at a low temperature of 5 °C, while being maintained under a nitrogen environment. The experimental procedure included the addition of 10 mg of ILIM (or alternatively, 20 mg of ILBIM) dissolved in DMSO to the reaction mixture. This addition took place at room temperature while maintaining continuous stirring for a duration of 24 h. Following the completion of the reaction, the resultant solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h, centrifuged for 10 min at 8000 rpm, and freeze-dried to obtain desired ionic liquid-capped CDs (ILIM@CDs or ILBIM@CDs). Functionalized ionic-liquid capped CDs would be further examined utilizing TEM, SEM, XPS, PXRD, Raman, FTIR, and UV-PL studies.

The high-resolution PXRD pattern of the fine-powdered samples were attained on a PANalytical Empyrean X-ray diffractometer at an accelerating potential (40 kV), in the 2θ range of 10–60° through a Cu Kα radiation source. Surface morphology and particle size of CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were evaluated via HRTEM on a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope with a 200 kV acceleration voltage. TEM samples were prepared by drop casting the material onto a carbon coated copper grid, followed by drying at room temperature. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) with EDS was done from University of Delhi using JEOL JSM 6610LV using Tungsten electron sources at 10KV with Magnification × 5 to × 3,00,000 with high vacuum mode using LN2 free EDS detector as well as from Amity University using Model Zeiss (EVO-18). 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded via a JEOL spectrometer using TMS as an internal standard. FTIR spectrums were obtained in the range 400–4000 cm−1, on a Nicolet-5DX FTIR spectrophotometer. Further, the elemental-composition analysis was attained via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectroscopy. XPS data was acquired with a PHI Versa Probe II with an Al Kα radiation. Raman spectroscopy was conducted at 50 × magnification using a confocal micro-Raman LabRam HR spectrometer (Horiba Scientific) with backscattering geometry, a CCD detector, and a 785 nm Ar laser. The optical and photoluminescence (PL) measurements for CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were monitored through Duetta Absorbance and Fluorescence Spectrometer in the wavelength range 200–800 nm using a xenon lamp as an excitation source.

Cell culture conditions and strains. The antibacterial activities were premeditated using ‘agar well diffusion’ and ‘macrodilution’ methods against Standard (E. coli (ATCC 25923)/ S. aureus (ATCC 25922)) and clinical multi-drug resistant bacterial strains from pus swab of diabetic foot ulcer. Initially the frozen bacteria were inoculated in logarithmic phase for 18 h in their desired growth medium at 37 °C to activate the metabolic procedure. OD measurements (at 600 nm) were used to calculate the bacterial concentration.

Antibacterial Screening. The agar well diffusion test was used for antibacterial screening. In brief, E. coli and S. aureus were uniformly inoculated on 100 mm diameter agar plates, and then wells were created in the agar plates using a sterile borer. The agar plates were supplemented with equal volumes (40 μL) of CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs through the constructed wells and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. Using the common scale, the diameter of the inhibitory zone in all samples was measured and represented as the mean standard deviation. All the experiments were carried out triplicate to calculate mean deviation.

Determination of Minimum Inhibitory Concentrations (MIC) by macrodilution method. The MIC values for CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs, and ILBIM@CDs were calculated using macrodilution method. Briefly, after autoclaving seven test tubes, 1 mL of sterile Mueller Hinton broth (MHB) medium was added to each one of the test tubes. Following that, 1 mL of as-prepared CDs-NH2 was added to the 1st test tube and thoroughly stirred to achieve a bubble-free solution, and transferred to a 2nd test tube, and so on until the seventh test tube was reached. In addition, all test tubes were inoculated with 5 μL of 1.5 × 107 CFU mL−1 (colony forming unit) diluted bacterial suspension and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h after adequate mixing. Furthermore, two test tubes were autoclaved and labelled M (medium), M + I (medium + inoculants) for controlled samples. All the control test tubes were incubated for 18 h at 37 °C. Further, this process was also conducted in a similar manner for the ionic-liquid capped CDs; ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs. Optical density (OD) measurements at 600 nm were used to assess bacterial growth. Finally, the MIC values of as-synthesized compounds were determined by taking the lowest sample concentration at which no detectable bacterial growth was found (no more than 1 %). The MIC values were determined using the following equation,

Additionally, to comprehending the additive effect of ILIM/ILBIM and CDs-NH2 in the ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs, the values of the fractional inhibition concentration (FIC) index were calculated. The following equation was used to calculate the simultaneous antibacterial activity of a and b (where a represents CDs-NH2, b represents ILIM/ILBIM, and ab represents ILIM@CDs/ILBIM@CDs):
FIC index
=

The FIC index's upper bound was determined in this study since MIC(a) and MIC(b) lower bounds were known. According to studies, FIC index's values between 0.5–2.0 have an additive effect, while values >0.5 have synergistic effects and values <2.0 have antagonistic effects.

The HEK-293T (human embryonic kidney) cell lines were incubated with 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotic (i.e., penicillin (100 U/mL) and streptomycin (100 μg/mL)) in DMEM-HG at 37 °C in a growth chamber with a CO2 supply of 5 %. Herein, the MTT assay was performed to determine the cytotoxic effects of ionic liquids (ILIM/ILBIM), CDs-NH2 and IL@CDs. Briefly, incubated HEK-293T cells (5 × 103 cells per well) were exposed to different concentrations of ILIM/ILBIM, CDs-NH2 and ILIM@CDs/ ILBIM@CDs for 24 h. The vehicle control cells were treated with 0.1 % deionized water. The samples' medium was then swapped with fresh media., followed by the addition of 20 mL of MTT (5 mg/mL) and incubation for further 4 h at 37 °C in the dark. OD measurements at 570 nm were used to assess% cell viability.

We performed molecular docking simulations of compounds (CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs, and ILBIM@CDs) at the DNA Gyrase receptor catalytic ligand binding site (PDBID: 6YD9) for the gram (-ive) bacteria, i. e. E. coli, and the SA DHFR receptor catalytic binding site of ligand (PDBID: 4TU5) for the gram (+ive) bacteria, i.e., S. aureus, respectively. The compound docking simulations (CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs, and ILBIM@CDs) were run using Maestro, version 9.6 from the Schrodinger software suite. The ligands were sketched out in 3D format using the build panel, and they were prepared for NMR docking using the ligprep tool. The protein used in the docking investigation was obtained from the Protein Data Bank (PDB ID: 6YD9) and (PDB ID: 4TU5), respectively, and created with the aid of the protein production wizard by removing the solvent, adding hydrogen, and further minimization while the standard ligand (Ridinilazole) was present. With bound co-crystallized ligand, grids for molecular docking were produced. The co-crystal ligand (ON2 and 06 W) was re-docked at the protein's catalytic site for the purpose of validating the docking parameters, and the RMSD between the co-crystal and re-docked poses was discovered to be 0.495 Å and 0.327 Å, respectively. Compound (CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs) was docked in Glide extra-precision (XP) mode, with a maximum of three postures preserved for each molecule.

CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were subjected to ADMET analysis by Qikprop tool. The descriptors calculated were blood brain barrier penetration analysis, lipophilicity, lead like properties and Lipinski rule parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs-NH2 and ionic liquid capped CDs (ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we developed a simple, user-friendly method for the hydrothermal synthesis of amorphous, highly fluorescent, biocompatible amine-modified carbon quantum dots (CDs-NH2) from inexpensive biowaste, specifically tecoma stans extract in the presence of ethylenediamine. As a result, the time-consuming multi-step processes have been avoided thanks to the use of tecoma stans extract as a carbon precursor. Hereafter, as-synthesized CDs-NH2 were covalently linked to the ionic liquids ILIM/ILBIM in existence of coupling reagents EDC/DMAP for 24 h. The structure of ILIM/ILBIM, CDs-NH2 and functionalized CDs i.e., IL@CDs asses as antibacterial agent are shown in Fig. 1 . The hydrothermal approach was used to synthesis CDs-NH2, which, in the aqueous phase, were discovered to be conductive and extremely stable, with no precipitation even after six months.

All samples were thoroughly characterized using a variety of characterization techniques, including XRD, HRTEM, SEM, FTIR, Raman, XPS, and PL analysis, to confirm the successful synthesis and reveal the intrinsic and morphological features of CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILIM@CDs/ILBIM@CDs). Initially, XRD analysis was used to clarify the morphological and structural characteristics of as-synthesised CDs-NH2 and ILIM@CDs/ILBIM@CDs. Typical PXRD of CDs-NH2 as shown in Fig. 2 a displays a broad (002) diffraction peak centered around 23.1° (2θ), indicating its amorphous nature based on (JCPDS No. 41–1487). The associated d -spacing is 3.5 Å, which is higher than the bulk graphite i.e., 3.3 Å, indicating that the sp2 carbon structure is deformed. However, after capping ionic-liquid (ILBIM) onto the surface of CDs-NH2 (ILBIM@CDs), the peak was slightly displaced 23.1 to 22.6° (2θ) with reducing the peak intensity of CDs-NH2 in ILBIM@CDs. The result shows the capping of ILBIM occurred on the CDs-NH2 surface, which consequently separated the inter layer space of ILBIM@CDs along with decreased peak intensity. As a result, the ILBIM@CDs XRD spectrum confirms the successful synthesis of ionic-liquid capped CDs.

The as-synthesized CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILBIM@CDs) were further characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) as depicted in Fig. S5 (see the supporting Information). Hereby, SEM morphology concluded that during carbonization process, graphitic rod is peeled away, and resulting carbon CDs-NH2 which is approximately spherical in nature, uniform in distribution and size, along with little-bit of agglomeration effect. The diameter of spherical shape of CDs-NH2 unit is about 5 nm confirmed by the TEM image as shown in Fig. 2(e). The high-resolution TEM images of CDs-NH2 were recorded by scanning the random positions onto the TEM grid and observed that CDs were uniform in size and monodisperse. Meanwhile, the spherical shape of CDs-NH2 unit is transformed into rod-shaped after capping of ionic-liquids on CDs-NH2 surface due to its ionic nature and also attributed to ᴨ-ᴨ stacking between N-atoms of imidazole rings,(Mishra et al., 2013, 2014b) which is also confirmed by the TEM image as shown in Fig. 2(d). The interlayer spacing or lattice fringes for CDs-NH2 and ILBIM@CDs are 0.35 nm and 0.37 nm, respectively (Fig. 2(e) and 2(f)). SEM and TEM images revealed that ILBIM is successfully fabricated on the surface of CDs-NH2.

FTIR, Raman and X-ray spectroscopy was accomplished to elucidate the elemental properties of as-synthesized CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs. In this context, in the presence of diverse surface functionalities, to investigate the successful production of CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs, FTIR spectra were taken. As depicted in Fig. 3 amine-modified CDs (CDs-NH2) displays vibrational band at 1363.8 cm−1 correspond to -CH2 stretching, which is equivalent to the carbon quantum dot's alkyl rocking.(Cayuela et al., 2016) The intense vibrational band at 1539.3 cm−1 represents surface -NH2 groups of CDs-NH2. In addition, a new stretching vibrational peak centered at around ∼1645.2 cm−1 includes the carbonyl groups of CDs-NH2. Moreover, a broad (less intense) band appears in the range of 3200–3400 cm−1 is ascribed to -OH and NH stretching vibrations of CDs-NH2, symbolizing their exceptional hydrophilicity. A peak centered at ∼1469.1 cm−1 and ∼1042.1 cm−1 is attributed to the C = C groups stretching vibrations in the aromatic rings and CO groups, respectively. Additionally, we observed a vibrational peak between 2500 and 3200 cm−1 which corresponds to OH and CH stretch of the corresponding acid groups in ILIM and ILBIM spectrum. Herein, to verify whether the ionic liquids on the CDs' surface had been effectively capped via amide coupling, we observed the occurrence of vibration peak at 1551.3 and 1546.3 cm−1 for amide -C-N stretching bands in ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs, respectively as well as peak centered at 2836.4 and 2935.9 cm−1 represents -CH2 stretching of the surface functionalized groups of ionic-liquids capped CDs for ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs respectively. Intriguingly, the reduction in the stretching vibration around ∼3100–3400 cm−1 indicates that the free -NH2 group is no longer accessible and is instead linked via amide linkage.(Cayuela et al., 2016) The presence of well-defined characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectra associated to several surface functional groups validates the successful capping of ionic liquids on the surface of CDs-NH2. Additionally, the extent to which the carbon matrix is structurally disordered was investigated qualitatively and quantitatively using Raman spectroscopy, a quasi-technique. Hereby, the Raman spectroscopy of CDs-NH2 and ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILBIM@CDs) was recorded (as depicted in Fig. 4 (a)). The Raman signals of the highly fluorescent CDs-NH2 were obscured by the background fluorescence, therefore the d- and G-bands for the synthesized CDs-NH2 in this work were not very noticeable. However, ionic-liquid capped CDs (ILBIM@CDs) recorded signals revealed two unique peaks at 1351 cm−1 and 1577 cm−1, which are associated with the characteristic D and G bands of carbon nanoparticles, pertaining to the disorder/defects and sp2 carbon in-plane vibration, respectively. The relative intensity ratio of D and G-band of ionic-liquid capped CDs is found to be 1.35, which is used to quantify the degree of sp3/sp2 hybridization changes. The creation of the two aforementioned signals and the substantial suppression of the CDs luminous background provided as evidence that the ionic-liquids were successfully capped on the CDs-NH2 surface.(Alarfaj et al., 2018)

To supplement the result of FTIR and Raman spectra, XPS spectroscopy was utilized to evaluate the elemental composition of CDs-NH2 and the ILBIM@CDs. XPS analysis of as-synthesized CDs-NH2 from tecoma stans flower and ILBIM@CDs shown in Fig. 5 . The survey scan of CDs-XPS in Fig. 4(b) shows that the aminated-CDs developed in presence of ethylenediamine from yellow-elder extract mostly consist of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen i.e., C1s (73.28 at.%), N1s (4.10 at.%) and O1s (22.50 at.%) respectively (as shown in Fig. 4(b)). The deconvolution of C1s peak is shown in Fig. 4(c), the main peak (CC/C = C) at 287.10 eV corresponds to sp2-graphitic structure whereas the peaks at 287.89 eV, 289.64 eV are assigned to CO and C = O, respectively. One more peak at 287.22 eV corresponds to CN shows that CDs-NH2 have a significant amount of amino functionalization.

The deconvolution of oxygen peak is shown in Fig. 4(e) at 533.67 eV also confirms the presence of CO/C = O, whereas 534.8 eV originated from isolated hydroxyl (OH). Fig. 4(d) also showing the peak of CN at 402.02 eV and NH at 403.28 eV confirms the functionalization of amino group on CDs-surface. Additionally, the presence of C, N and O is also confirmed by EDAX spectrum (Fig. S6; see the supporting Information). The survey scan of ILBIM@CDs-XPS shows the composition of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen i.e., C1s (86.28 at.%), N1s (8.22 at.%) and O1s (5.50 at.%) respectively (Fig. 4(b)) and confirmed that the increased percentage composition of C and N is due to the functionalization of CDs-NH2 with ILBIM. The deconvolution of C1s peak as shown in Fig. 4(f) confirms the capping of ILBIM on CDs-NH2 surface by showing the peaks of -C-C-/-C = C-, -C-N-, -C-O-, -N-C = O and -OCO, at 287.17 eV, 287.53 eV, 288.04 eV, 289.11 eV and 290.72 eV, respectively. The -NCO peak confirms the presence of amide linkage between CDs-NH2 and ILBIM (as shown in Fig. 4(f)). Further, the deconvolution of N1s peaks as shown in Fig. 4(g), the main peak appeared (CN-C) at 401.22 eV corresponds to amide linkage in ILBIM@CDs, confirms the attachment of ILBIM on CDs-NH2.

Further, to better understand optical behavior of synthesized ILIM, ILBIM, CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs, UV–Visible as well as fluorescence spectroscopy were performed. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs-NH2 showed λmax at 340 nm corresponding to n-ᴨ* transition; though, the spectrum of ILIM and ILBIM shows broad edges around ∼347 nm which corresponds ᴨ-ᴨ* transition. Meanwhile, ionic-liquid capped CDs; ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs, show absorbance maxima at 344 and 345 nm, respectively. The shoulder's peak ranges at 300–400 nm were ascribed to n-ᴨ* transition of C = O (Gan et al., 2013) (Fig. 5(a)). Under ambient light, the CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs have excellent water dispersibility and a light-yellow color, while UV irradiation with a 340–350 nm trans-illuminator produces a fair blue fluorescence. When stimulated at 360 nm, both ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs exhibit emission maxima at 457 and 470 nm, respectively, in the fluorescence spectra that demonstrate the efficient capping of ionic liquids on the surface of CDs. Further, excitation-dependent emission was observed for CDs-NH2 in aqueous medium, which is a superficial feature of CDs. CDs-NH2 emission profile was recorded at varied wavelengths ranging from 360 to 440 nm (as depicted in Fig. 5(b)). An increase in the excitation wavelength was accompanied by a bathochromic (red) shift in the emission wavelength (for both CDs-NH2 and ILBIM@CDs, Fig. 5(c) & 5(d)), along with fluorescence quenching. Numerous theories have been put out, but the mechanism underlying such a process is still unclear. According to some reports in the literature, this is due to the small particles size, as well as the quantum confinement effect. Others believe that during carbonization, the ratio of sp3/sp2 hybridization changes, resulting in the formation of hole pairs, which explains the luminescence behavior of these CDs. (Li et al., 2010; Lim et al., 2015; Oza et al., 2016) Additionally, because of the ampholyte groups, which have changing isoelectric points and dissociation constants, in addition, are extremely sensitive to pH, such as carboxylic and phenolic hydroxyl, etc., binding to the surface of CDs. Therefore, it is reasonable to anticipate the pH-dependent emission behavior. The emission dramatically enhanced when the pH was raised from 1.07 to 7.09, according to the findings given in Fig. 5(f) & 5(e). The emission gradually decreased as pH gradually rose to 12.29. (Fig. 5(f)). Both represent the combined reaction to pH of the ampholytes groups on the surface of CDs-NH2. Concluded that, compared to alkaline and acidic pH, the steady performance of PL was at its maximum at neutral pH. In addition, the quantum yield of as-synthesized CDs-NH2 was found to be 27.48 % using quinine sulfate as a reference, which is acceptable when compared to other previously reported green synthesized CDs (Table 1 ). Table 1 summarizes and compares the characteristics of various precursor-derived CDs in terms of QY, reaction conditions, elemental content, and optical properties to correlate the features of CDs produced by different green biomass precursors.(Alamdari et al., 2023; Alshammari et al., 2023; Atchudan et al., 2022; Bressi et al., 2022; Ng et al., 2021; Rabiee et al., 2022) As, proposed mechanism of synthesis of CDs from biomass, concluded that hydrolysis and dehydration of biomass during hydrothermal treatment, refurbished into aromatic moieties. Moreover, based on different evaluation techniques mentioned in Table 1, the presence of EDA assist in the dehydration of polysaccharides isolated from biowaste, which remarked enhanced QY of as-synthesized CDs. (Sharma et al., 2019)

In conclusion, the current study demonstrates the synthesis of CDs-NH2 from tecoma stans biomass that exhibit remarkable optical properties, such as a significant quantum yield, persistent fluorescence, and wavelength-controlled emission. Green precursors or biomass have benefits in terms of sustainability, affordability, and mass synthesis, emerging as a possible candidate for many applications.

The ionic-liquid capped CDs-NH2 (ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs) were employed to avert bacterial growth by taking advantage of their ultrasmall size. The potential of as-synthesized functionalized CDs; ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs as a next-generation antibacterial agent with high biocompatibility was examined at several concentrations against standard (E. coli; Gram(-ive)/S. aureus; Gram(+ive)) and clinical multi-drug resistant bacteria using the macro-dilution method. Additionally, various concentrations of ILIM, ILBIM, and CDs-NH2 were used to compare and evaluate the antibacterial activity of the various functionalized CDs' constituent components. Initially, the macrodilution approach was used to investigate antibacterial behavior. Once the MIC value was established, the values for the FIC index were determined. Table 2 summarizes the MIC values of ILIM, ILBIM, CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs needed to inhibit E. coli and S. aureus. Table 2 demonstrates that the MIC values of 64 and 16 μg mL−1 of the ILBIM@CDs are sufficient to inhibit the growth of E. coli and S. aureus respectively. In contrast, the associated MIC values of ILIM@CDs were found to be slightly higher i.e., 32 and 16 μg mL−1, respectively, concluded the superior antibacterial behavior of ILBIM@CDs over ILIM@CDs due to presence of phenyl ring. In comparison, the MIC values of ILIM, ILBIM and CDs-NH2 were found to be substantially higher, exceeding >64, >64, >256 μg mL−1 and >64, >64, >128 μg mL−1 respectively. This significant enhancement in the MIC values enables us to conclude that the as-synthesized ionic-liquid capped CDs; ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs exhibit enhanced antibacterial activity over CDs-NH2 and ILBIM/ ILIM respectively. Similarly, the MIC values of ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were found to be 250 μg mL−1, slightly lower as compared to that of CDs-NH2 (>1000 μg mL−1), demonstrated enhanced antibacterial activity of ILIM@CDs/ILBIM@CDs against multi-drug resistant clinical resistant bacterial pathogen.

Furthermore, the concentration-dependent investigation of a range of concentrations 1024, 512, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16, 8 and 4 μg mL−1 had been taken for standard bacterial pathogens. Hereby results concluded that, ILBIM@CDs/ ILIM@CDs demonstrated enhanced antibacterial activity compared to CDs-NH2/ILBIM/ILIM and showed significant effect against highly suspectable E. coli ATCC 25923 strains. In case of E. coli, ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs has demonstrated to be a more effective inhibitor of bacterial growth at concentration of 64 and 32 μg mL−1 which is also concluded by molecular docking studies when compared to standard drug Ridinilazole along with standard antibacterial drug available in market (ampicillin, erythromycin, and streptomycin).(Cui et al., 2021; Garcia et al., 2013) The E. coli viability leaps from 98.10±1.03 % at the ILBIM@CDs concentration of 4 μg mL−1 to 4.01±0.50 % at 32 μg mL−1. In case of ILIM@CDs, viability leaps jump from 100.5 ± 0.06 % at the ILIM@CDs concentration of 8 μg mL−1 to 3.84±0.56 % at 64 μg mL−1. On a similar note, CDs-NH2 at the concentration of 128 μg mL, shows E. coli viability 67.9 ± 0.68 %, which is extremely higher as compared to cell viability 2.9 ± 0.591 % of CDs-NH2 at S. aureus, concluded poor antibacterial activity of CDs-NH2 over E. coli as compared to S. aureus, which is also manifested by molecular docking studies. In a similar manner, ILIM@CDs S. aureus viability leaps from 90.48±1.38% at the concentration of 4 μg mL−1 to 6.06±0.99 % at 16 μg mL−1. In comparison to ILIM@CDs, ILBIM@CDs shows superior antibacterial activity where cell viability jumps from 87.25±1.38 % at the concentration of 4 μg mL−1 to 2.60±0.09 % at 16 μg mL−1. Similarly, the concentration-dependent investigation of a range of concentrations 1000, 500, 250, 125, 62.5, 31.25, 15.65 and 7.81 μg mL−1 had been taken for clinical multi-drug resistant bacterial strain. In this regard, the results concluded that ILBIM@CDs illustrated enhanced antibacterial activity, by inhibiting the bacterial growth at concentration 250 μg mL−1, 4-times lower than that of CDs-NH2. The clinical multi-drug resistant bacterial strain viability leaps from 99.27±0.47 % at the ILBIM@CDs concentration of 31.25 μg mL−1 to 6.01±0.32 % at 250 μg mL−1.

The upper bound FIC index values were then evaluated using the MIC values of CDs-NH2 and ILBIM/ILIM as the lower bound in order to comprehend any potential additive effect of CDs-NH2 and ILBIM/ILIM in ionic-liquid capped CDs; ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs. FIC index's values that have been determined (ILBIM@CDs; 0.625 for E. coli and 0.375 for S. aureus, ILIM@CDs; 1.25 for E. coli and 0.375 for S. aureus) was found between 0.5 to 2.0 for E. coli, and value below 0.5 for S. aureus, thereby signifying the presence of additive effect between CDs-NH2 and ILBIM/ILIM for Gram(-ive) bacteria and synergistic effect for Gram(+ive) bacteria. Ionic-liquid capped CDs could therefore be applied in a range of biological applications as a next-generation antibacterial agent.

In addition, the agar well diffusion method (Fig. S7; see the supporting Information) was used to evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of the CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs, as well as to validate the macrodilution method's results. Table 3 summarizes the measured zone of inhibition, diameter for CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs diffusion at the same concentration (256 μg mL−1). It can be observed from Table 3 that ILBIM@CDs shows maximum inhibition showing superior antibacterial efficiency as compared to ILIM@CDs and CDs-NH2 at 256 μg mL−1. Comparatively, it can be observed that at 100 μg mL−1, ILIM, ILBIM, ILIM@CDs, ILBIM@CDs hindered the growth of both Gram(+ive) and Gram(-ive) bacteria, while no inhibition was shown by CDs-NH2 at a concentration lower than their MIC values. Since no inhibition zone was seen in the case of CDs-NH2, statistical analysis is not required to be done. Using the Student's t-test, the statistical analysis of the inhibitory zones obtained for the two bacteria is done in the case ionic-liquids (ILIM and ILBIM) and ionic-liquid-capped CDs (ILIM@CDs/ ILBIM@CDs). A considerable difference in efficacy between the two bacterial strains can be seen in the calculated p-value between S. aureus and E. coli, which is < 0.01–0.05. These findings demonstrate that when compared to ILIM/ILBIM and CDs-NH2, the ILIM@CDs/ ILBIM@CDs might serve as a more effective antibacterial agent. Similarly, for clinical multi-drug resistant bacteria strain, ILBIM@CDs shows maximum zone of inhibition at 500 μg mL−1, exceedingly as compared to ILIM@CDs while CDs-NH2 shows no zone of inhibition at a concentration lower than their MIC value.

Furthermore, we examined the time dependent antibacterial activity of as-synthesized CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs against both standard (Gram(+ive)/Gram(-ive)) and clinical multi-drug resistant bacterial strain. The outcome of CDs-NH2 on E. coli bacterial growth is shown in Fig. 6 (a), and even after 1 h of incubation, appreciable growth is seen (as concentration below MIC), considerably less, as compared to control. However, in the case of ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs presence of prolonged lag phase, along with negligible bacterial growth that lasts for 1.5 h (Fig. 6(b)) and 2 h (Fig. 6(c)), respectively. Hence, ionic-liquid capped CDs exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity, as the exponential growth after 2 h of incubation was later discovered to be much slower than that of the control. Similarly, in case of S. aureus, CDs-NH2 (Fig. 6(d)) exhibited prolonged lag phase, however short after incubation of 1 h, indicates more sensitivity of as-synthesized CDs-NH2 towards Gram(+ive) bacteria as compared to Gram(-ive) bacteria. Subsequently, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity along with prolonged phases up to 2 h (Fig. 6(e) & 6(f)) after incubation. Hereafter, clinical multi-drug resistant bacterial strain collected from diabetic pus swab were examined. Both ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs showed prolonged phase up to 2 h (Fig. 6(h) & 6(i)), showed enhanced antibacterial activity as compared to that of CDs-NH2.

As shown by the agar well-diffusion method (Table 3), since no inhibition zone was seen in the case of CDs-NH2 (at 100 µg mL−1), statistical analysis is not required to be done. Using the student's t-test, the statistical analysis of the inhibitory zones obtained for the two bacteria is done in the case ionic-liquids (ILIM and ILBIM) and ionic-liquid-capped CDs (ILIM@CDs/ ILBIM@CDs). For ILIM@CDs/ ILBIM@CDs, a considerable difference in efficacy between the two bacterial strains can be seen in the calculated p-value (estimated value; 0.0013) between S. aureus and E. coli, which is < 0.01. These findings demonstrate that when compared to ILIM/ILBIM and CDs-NH2, the ILIM@CDs/ ILBIM@CDs might serve as a more effective antibacterial agent.

Given the existing cumulative effect of ILIM@CDs and CDs-NH2, the study presented above clearly shows that the ionic-liquid capped ILBIM@CDs have superior antibacterial activity to that of ILIM@CDs and CDs-NH2, as shown by the comparison and FIC index values. In order to fully understand the antibacterial mechanism of these ILBIM@CDs, it is essential to discuss any potential interactions between these molecules and bacteria. While the exact mechanism underlying the antibacterial activity of ILBIM@CDs/CDs-NH2 is unknown, a variety of potential interactions between bacteria and functionalized-CDs, including interactions with ionic liquids, have been proposed to explain the inhibitory mechanism. It has been reported that the overall phenomenon of cell wall destruction in Gram(-ive) and Gram(+ive) bacteria via ionic liquid penetration, is attributed to the disruption of biochemical gradients between the cytoplasm and the outer environment after ionic liquid exposure, causing intracellular content to diffuse or extracellular material to enter the cytoplasm.(Nikfarjam et al., 2021b) In case of carbon quantum dots, the inhibitory activity primarily accomplished through the three molecular pathways; (1) cell wall/cell membrane destruction, (2) formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) to damage cells, (3) binding interactions with nucleic material (DNA/RNA) to avert cell growth.(Rajendiran et al., 2019) Some report suggested that the discriminatory antibacterial activity of CDs, may be attribute to the variation in the structural and chemical properties of the cell walls. Gram(+ive) bacteria's cell walls are made up of a thick but simple single-layered membrane (composed of peptidoglycan as well as teichoic acids), suggesting that CDs and Gram(+ive) bacteria may interact more favorably because of their permeable cell walls. Additionally, the CDs were found to be attached on specific sites on the surface of Gram(+ive) bacteria. (Yang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019) Unlike Gram(-ive) bacteria, the cell structural collapse of Gram(+ive) bacteria was caused by the disintegration of the cytoplasmic membrane caused by the penetration of CDs. CDs can approach and penetrate Gram(+ive) bacteria due to presence of polar groups on the surface and accommodate small size, causing changes in cell permeability and intracellular component leakage, ultimately resulting in cellular harm and death. The cell membrane of Gram(-ive) bacteria is made up of a double-layered membrane and is more sophisticated than that of gram(+ive) bacteria.(Han et al., 2017) Gram(-ive) bacteria's outer membrane and intermittent peptidoglycan network might result in a weak-interaction with CDs, due to which some CDs shows almost negligible antibacterial effect against Gram(-ive) bacteria. Frequently, it has been claimed that photo-excited CDs are able to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) that interfere with the production of biofilm and rupture the cell walls of both Gram(+ive) and Gram(-ive) bacteria, resulting in cell lysis. Concluded that ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs can generate ROS formation to a greater extent when compared with CDs-NH2 and ILBIM/ILIM on both bacterial strains due to presence of additive effect. These results suggest that ionic-liquid capped CDs; ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs shows efficient antibacterial activity on both strains of bacteria. Hence, the above discussed mechanism of functionalized CDs; ILBIM@CDs/ ILIM@CDs may occur and result in improved antibacterial activity of the ILBIM@CDs/ ILIM@CDs as compared to ILBIM/ILIM and CDs-NH2.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of synthesized ionic-liquid capped CDs; ILBIM@CDs/ ILIM@CDs, we have studied the cytotoxic effect of ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs toward mammalian cells. As a result, all compounds (ILIM, ILBIM, CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs) were tested for biocompatibility on HEK-293T (human embryonic kidney 293) cell lines, as shown in Fig. 7 , since many nanoparticles are discovered to be harmful to the majority of human cells. Distinct vary concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL−1) of all the compounds containing (5 × 103 cells per well) HEK-293T cells were supplemented at 37 °C for 24 h. The viability of cells was observed to be reduced by roughly 21.02, 23.27, 0.35, 0.57 and 0.96 % following lowest concentration (25 μg mL−1) of ILIM, ILBIM, CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs respectively. Interestingly, ionic-liquid capped CDs; ILBIM@CDs/ ILIM@CDs at concentration >200 μg mL−1, HEK-293T cell line show minimal cytotoxicity response (cell viability >90 % with varying potencies p-value), which are ∼200-fold greater than the MIC tested for bacteria. In comparison to bacterial cell membranes, mammalian cell membranes are mostly composed of lipids with a lower net charge and are higher in cholesterol. This suggests that the improved selective inhibitory effect of ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs on bacteria is caused by a special interaction between ionic-liquid capped CDs and bacterial cell membranes. This pattern of cytotoxicity and antibacterial activities of ionic-liquid capped CDs suggests that both ILBIM@CDs/ILIM@CDs, might be a potent antibacterial agent in the future.

Similarly, to elucidate the antibacterial behavior of synthesized CDs-NH2, ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs in reference to antibacterial drug Ridinilazole for Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus bacteria docking studies have been conducted against SA-DHFR receptor (Organism: Staphylococcus aureus) and the docking studies reveal that the compounds (CDs-NH2, ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs) have a similar binding orientation of compounds in the SA-DHFR receptor's catalytic binding pocket (PDBID: 4TU5). Herein, we report the binding pose of CDs-NH2, ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs; and compare with the standard ligand (Ridinazole) which was docked against SA-DHFR protein. CDs-NH2, ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs bind in catalytic domain of SA-DHFR receptor. ILBIM@CDs binding interaction (Hydrogen Bonding) with backbone of SA-DHFR receptor with Leu20, Phe92 and Lys32. CDs-NH2, ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs superimposition with standard ligand (Ridinilazole) ligand and showed same interaction was found in catalytic domain of SA-DHFR receptor as shown in Fig. 9 , Table 4). Additionally, enhanced antibacterial activity of CDs-NH2, ILBIM@CDs and ILIM@CDs against standard drug; Ridinilazole, have been suggested based on docking score i.e., −6.782, −5.959, −5.515 and −3.947 respectively.

CDs-NH2, ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were subjected to ADMET analysis by qikprop. The descriptors calculated were blood brain barrier (BBB) penetration analysis, lipophilicity, lead like properties and Lipinski rule parameters were tabulated in the Table 5 . From the study it was observed that almost all the hits follow Lipinski rule but violates lead likeness properties, BBB permeability.

We have successfully synthesized novel biocompatible carbon quantum dots (CDs) capped with ionic-liquids, namely ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs utilizing fresh flowers of Tecoma stans (L.) Juss. ex Kanth. These CDs capped with ionic-liquids materials were prepared using carbiimide chemistry, resulting in uniform decoration of ILIM and ILBIM onto amine-modified carbon quantum dots. Our characterization findings confirm the effective capping of ionic liquids onto CDs-NH2, ensuring the stability and degradability of ILIM @CDs/ILBIM@CDs. These capped CDs exhibited significantly improved antibacterial properties compared to CDs-NH2 and standalone ILIM/ILBIM. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values for ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs were as low as >64 μg mL−1 and 16 μg mL−1, respectively, effectively preventing the growth of model bacterial strains E. coli and S. aureus. Importantly, ILIM@CDs/ILBIM@CDs also demonstrated enhanced antibacterial activity against clinical multi-drug resistant bacterial strains, with MIC values four times lower than those of CDs-NH2. In addition to their superior antibacterial properties, ILIM@CDs/ILBIM@CDs exhibited higher cell viability when tested on HEK-293T cell lines. This indicates their biocompatibility and potential for safe use in biomedical applications. Taken together, the enhanced antibacterial activity and high cell viability results suggest that ILIM@CDs/ILBIM@CDs hold great promise as advanced antibacterial agents for a wide range of biomedical applications. These applications include the development of textiles for use as wound dressings, antibacterial coatings, disinfectants, and various healthcare products.

In summary, our study highlights the successful synthesis of biocompatible ILIM@CDs and ILBIM@CDs with enhanced antibacterial properties and high cell viability, making them promising candidates for a variety of biomedical and healthcare applications.

In the supporting information 1HNMR and 13CNMR spectrum of as-synthesized ionic-liquids, EDX spectrum of synthesized CDs, antibacterial activity and report of clinical multi-drug resistant bacteria obtained from diabetic foot-pus swabs are provided.

Nisha Yadav: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Deeksha Mudgal: Investigation, Formal analysis. Shivakant Mishra: Resources. Hitesh Sehrawat: Investigation, Formal analysis. Niraj Kumar Singh: Resources. Kalicharan Sharma: Resources. Prabodh Chander Sharma: Resources. Jay Singh: Resources. Vivek Mishra: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision.
